Know exactly why you'd sell
before you buy anything.

Farson is a personal investment research platform built on the same underwriting framework used by Apollo, KKR, and Blackstone — applied to your own portfolio.

IC Memo on every position
Written thesis, kill-switch, valuation anchor — before any capital moves
AI Investment Committee
Claude runs forensic analysis on your portfolio using the Apollo framework
Energy sector thesis
Full commodity cycle view — E&P, majors, midstream — built in
4 quick questions · No finance knowledge needed · 45 seconds
Roman Boschetto
SMU Cox School of Business · Finance & Economics · AAMC Candidate · Energy Private Equity
+1 (801) 755-0851
Skip →
Question 1 of 3
You have $10,000 to invest. What matters most?
No right or wrong answer — just pick what feels right.
💵
It pays me cash every year
I want income I can see and count on
Warren Buffett's approach. Own great businesses that generate cash.
🚀
It grows as fast as possible
I can wait — I want the biggest return
Amazon, Tesla territory. High risk, high reward, long time horizon.
🛡️
I don't lose it
Safety first — I'd rather sleep well than gamble
Capital preservation. The first rule of professional investing: never lose money.
Question 2 of 3
A stock you own drops 30% overnight. What do you do?
The market just crashed. Your portfolio is down. Clock is ticking.
🚪
Sell — something must be wrong
Cut the loss before it gets worse
The most common move. Also how most people lose money — selling fear instead of facts.
⏸️
Wait and see what happens
Don't panic — let the dust settle
Patient and disciplined. Most of the time the right call — noise clears, value remains.
🛒
Buy more — same business, cheaper price
If nothing changed about the company, the drop is a gift
High-conviction investing. The hardest move — and statistically the most profitable.
Question 3 of 3
One rule separates every professional investor from everyone else.
Before they buy anything, they write down exactly what would make them sell it.
📉
"I'll sell if the price drops 15%"
A number-based exit
This is emotion dressed as a rule. Price is noise — the business is what matters.
🎯
"I'll sell if the reason I bought changes"
A logic-based exit tied to the business, not the stock price
This is how the best investors think. Called a kill-switch. Written before the trade is made.
See it in action
Here's what that looks like on a real company.
EOG Resources — one of America's largest oil producers. Watch the AI apply the rule.
AI Investment Committee — analyzing EOG Resources
Tap the button below to run the analysis.

What this is

A personal public markets platform built to practise the discipline I want to apply in energy private equity — investment committee memos, conviction-weighted allocation, and forensic thesis review on a live portfolio.

Every position carries a written IC memo: dominant question, variant perception, quantified downside, catalyst path, and an explicit kill-switch threshold. Built independently — no template, no course requirement.

Career thesis
Target path: AAMC → energy investment banking (Tudor Pickering Holt / Goldman NR) → energy private equity
Background: Idaho Helicopters (family aviation ops) — firsthand view of how energy companies operate and procure capital equipment
Strategic allocation targets: 25–30% Energy · 15–20% Alt Managers · 20–25% Semiconductors — sized around a 30-year compounding horizon
LSE London Summer 2027 · Ironman Texas April 2029
Built independently · No template · All thesis memos written by the operator · IC analysis powered by Claude (Anthropic) · Apollo / Marc Rowan underwriting framework

Energy Sector

Commodity cycle thesis · Sub-sector architecture · 25–30% portfolio target
—
Commodity Cycle Thesis
Reviewed May 2025
Macro View — Why Energy, Why Now

The post-2020 capex retrenchment by majors and independents created a structural supply deficit that persists even as demand proves more resilient than the energy transition narrative implies. WTI in the $65–85 range is the base case through 2027 — above the $50–55 shale breakeven for tier-1 Permian operators, and below the demand destruction threshold. At these prices, best-in-class E&P names generate 8–10% FCF yields and return 60–75% of free cash flow to shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

US LNG export capacity is growing 15%+ through 2027 (Sabine Pass T6, Golden Pass, Plaquemines), creating a structural demand floor for domestic natural gas and lifting midstream throughput. The energy transition is real but long-dated — global oil demand does not peak in base-case scenarios before 2030, and the grid buildout for electrification is itself energy-intensive.

Variant View · What the Market Gets Wrong
Supply discipline is stickier than consensus: US shale growth has structurally decelerated — investor pressure for returns over volume means the 2014–2019 growth-at-all-costs model is gone. OPEC+ cohesion is higher than it looks because member state fiscal breakevens have risen.
ESG outflow pressure creates mispricing: Passive ESG flows have systematically underweighted energy for 5 years. This creates a valuation gap — quality operators like EOG and COP trade at 10–11x forward earnings vs 18–22x for equivalent-quality compounders in other sectors.
Kill switch: WTI sustained below $55 for 2+ quarters, OR shale productivity rebounds materially, OR OPEC+ cohesion breaks with significant quota violations. Any of these would trigger full position review.

75 / 100
FCF Yield

Buy Zone

Midstream
Fee-based cash flows · commodity-lite
WMB Williams Companies
Accumulate
Conviction
80 / 100
DCF Yield
~6%
Buy Zone
$50–$60
Transco pipeline — largest nat gas pipeline in the US. 96%+ fee-based revenue. LNG export buildout = structural throughput growth.
Why Midstream in an Equity Portfolio

Fee-based midstream is the bond-like anchor of the energy sector — 95%+ of revenue is contractual, volumes are take-or-pay, and cash flows compound regardless of commodity prices. WMB specifically benefits from the LNG export buildout: every new Gulf Coast LNG terminal moves gas through Transco.

Energy Universe · Summary
6 names · 3 sub-sectors
Ticker Name Sub-sector Conviction Signal FCF Yield Buy Zone One-line thesis
XOM ExxonMobil Integrated Major 87 Strong Buy ~5% $105–$130 Pioneer + Guyana = decade of low-cost production growth
CVX Chevron Integrated Major 70 Hold ~5.5% $145–$175 Hess adds Guyana; await better entry below $155
EOG EOG Resources E&P 88 Strong Buy ~9% $110–$130 Best-in-class F&D cost; $50 breakeven; 15yr tier-1 inventory
COP ConocoPhillips E&P 84 Accumulate ~7% $95–$115 Multi-basin diversification reduces single-commodity risk
CTRA Coterra Energy E&P 75 Accumulate ~8% $22–$30 Oil/gas optionality; LNG export upside via Marcellus nat gas
WMB Williams Companies Midstream 80 Accumulate ~6% $50–$60 Transco pipeline; 96% fee-based; LNG throughput tailwind
